---
title: "When Models Become Art Forgers"
subtitle: "A case study with Greg Rutkowski and generative models"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-title: "Contents"
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    page-layout: article
    smooth-scroll: true
    html-math-method: katex
---

## The question

When a generative model learns to paint like a specific artist, is it forging their work? The word is loaded, but the phenomenon is real. Models trained on an artist's images can reproduce their visual style on demand, without credit, consent, or compensation. Whether that constitutes forgery in any legal sense is still being decided in court.

What is less discussed is what the artist can actually find out. If you suspect a model was trained on your work, what tools do you have? This post works through that question using Greg Rutkowski as a case study, moving across three levels of access:

- **The training data.** If the dataset is public, you can search it directly.
- **The model weights.** If the model is open-weight, you can probe its internals.
- **The model outputs.** Even without weights, you can generate images and measure them.

After working through all three, we ask a more practical question: can someone actually reproduce the artist's style on demand today, with commercially available models? And are those models trying to prevent it?

---

## Greg Rutkowski

Greg Rutkowski is a Polish digital artist whose paintings hang in the portfolios of Dungeons and Dragons, Magic: The Gathering, Sony, and Ubisoft. His work is immediately recognizable: dramatic side-lighting, rich ochre and crimson palettes, and a classical oil-painting texture applied to fantastical subjects.

```{=html}
<div class="portfolio-carousel">
  <button class="carousel-btn" id="prevBtn" onclick="carouselMove(-1)">&#8249;</button>
  <div class="carousel-track-wrap">
    <div class="carousel-track" id="carouselTrack"></div>
  </div>
  <button class="carousel-btn" id="nextBtn" onclick="carouselMove(1)">&#8250;</button>
</div>
<p class="carousel-caption">Selected works from Greg Rutkowski's portfolio. Use arrows to browse all 30 paintings.</p>
<script>
const portfolioImages = [
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-after-battle-final-last.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-avatar-of-growth.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-castle-defence-1920.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-demon-berserker-1200.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-dragonslayer-key-art-final-1920.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-enhanced-surveillance-1500.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-firemind-s-research.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-fisherman-boy-1500.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-ghosts-of-saltmarsh-1920.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-god-eternal-oketra-1600.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-grim-draugr-1500.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-gutterbones-1600.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-howlpack-avenger-1600.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-immersturm-raider-1200.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-karakter-design-studio-1800-key-plantage.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-karakter-design-studio-1800-key-revolution.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-karakter-design-studio-1800-key-worldexpo.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-karakter-kar-ntr-aloy-encounter.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-karakter-kar-ntr-utr-arc-underneath.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-lw-promotional-illustration-final-2-1920-2.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-mind-drain-1600.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-monarch-ceremony-illustration-final-1920-last.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-monsters-of-the-multiverse-1920.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-rot-hulk.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-study-42-1200.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-sunset-study-1600.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-tibalt-cosmic-impostor-1200.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-vanquish-the-horde-1500.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-vraska-scheming-gorgon-1200.jpg",
  "assets/portfolio/greg-rutkowski-winter-river-study-1500.jpg"
];
const PER_PAGE = 3;
let page = 0;
function renderCarousel() {
  const track = document.getElementById('carouselTrack');
  track.innerHTML = '';
  const start = page * PER_PAGE;
  for (let i = start; i < Math.min(start + PER_PAGE, portfolioImages.length); i++) {
    const div = document.createElement('div');
    div.className = 'carousel-item';
    const img = document.createElement('img');
    img.src = portfolioImages[i];
    img.alt = 'Greg Rutkowski painting';
    div.appendChild(img);
    track.appendChild(div);
  }
  document.getElementById('prevBtn').disabled = page === 0;
  document.getElementById('nextBtn').disabled = (page + 1) * PER_PAGE >= portfolioImages.length;
}
function carouselMove(dir) { page = Math.max(0, Math.min(page + dir, Math.ceil(portfolioImages.length / PER_PAGE) - 1)); renderCarousel(); }
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', renderCarousel);
</script>
```

In 2022, users of Stable Diffusion discovered that appending "by Greg Rutkowski" to any prompt reliably upgraded the output. The phrase became more common in SD prompts than "by Picasso" or "by Da Vinci." His name appeared in more than 93,000 public prompts in a single month. He learned about this from journalists, not from Stability AI.

He is now a named plaintiff in [*Andersen v. Stability AI*](https://www.courtlistener.com/docket/66732129/andersen-v-stability-ai-ltd/) (N.D. Cal. 3:23-cv-00201), a class action alleging that Stability AI trained its models on billions of scraped images without consent. In November 2025, the UK High Court ruled in [*Getty Images v. Stability AI*](https://www.bailii.org/ew/cases/EWHC/Ch/2025/2863.html) that "the Model itself does not store any of those Copyright Works," narrowing but not ending the debate.

His case is well-suited to this investigation. His work is confirmed in LAION-5B, the training dataset for Stable Diffusion 1.x. Stability AI reportedly removed him from SD 2.1's training data. And his ongoing lawsuit makes the evidentiary question legally consequential, not merely academic.

---

## Level 1: The training data

The most direct question is also the easiest: is the artist's work actually in the training data?

LAION-5B is a public index of 5.85 billion image-text pairs scraped from the web, and the training data for Stable Diffusion 1.x. It stores URL metadata and CLIP embeddings for each image — not the images themselves. The intended way to search it is by embedding: compute a CLIP ViT-L/14 embedding for a portfolio image, query the LAION knn index, and retrieve nearest neighbors by cosine similarity. A match above 0.95 cosine similarity means the image, or a near-duplicate, was in the training data at collection time. This is exact enough to say with confidence whether a specific painting was scraped.

In practice, running this search today is no longer possible. LAION stores only URLs, and the web has moved on: by 2026, a large fraction of those URLs return 404s or have been actively taken down, in part because of the litigation. The retrieval service that hosted the knn index (knn.laion.ai) is also offline. What remains is metadata — captions, URLs, embeddings — without the images they pointed to.

Rutkowski's presence in LAION-5B is not in dispute. It is documented in the lawsuit record, confirmed by the [Somepalli et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.01292) study, and acknowledged by Stability AI. His name appeared in an estimated 93,000 publicly shared prompts in a single month in 2022, a figure that traces back to images scraped and captioned under his name. The infrastructure to verify this independently is no longer accessible, but the fact itself is established.

This establishes **access** in the copyright sense: the defendant had the opportunity to copy the work. Whether that access translates into a detectable signal inside the trained model is a harder question — and the one we turn to next.

---

## Level 2: The model weights

Since SD 1.5 is open-weight, we can load it and inspect its internal behavior. The question is whether training on Rutkowski's images left a detectable trace in the weights.

The standard approach is **loss-based membership inference**, developed for diffusion models by [Matsumoto et al. (IEEE S&P Workshops 2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.03262). Diffusion models are trained to predict the noise added to an image at each timestep:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{t,\, x_0,\, \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)} \left[ \bigl\| \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t,\, t) \bigr\|^2 \right]$$

If the model trained on an image, it should denoise it more accurately, producing lower prediction error. We measure that error across six timesteps for each portfolio image and use the mean as a membership score. The control group is **Charlie Bowater**, a UK-based fantasy illustrator working in a comparable genre. If Rutkowski is a training member, his images should have lower loss than Bowater's — the model should denoise what it has seen before.

```{=html}
<script src="https://cdn.plot.ly/plotly-2.26.0.min.js"></script>
<div class="plotly-wrap">
  <div id="fig2a"></div>
  <p class="fig-caption"><strong>Figure 2a.</strong> Mean prediction MSE vs. timestep for Rutkowski (red) and Bowater (blue) on SD 1.5 (left) and SD 2.1 (right). Each panel has its own y-axis; the models are not directly comparable across panels. Shaded bands show ±1 SD.</p>
</div>
<script>
function loadPlotly(id, jsonPath, overrides) {
  fetch(jsonPath).then(r => r.json()).then(fig => {
    const layout = Object.assign({}, fig.layout, {
      title: '',
      margin: {t: 30, b: 50, l: 55, r: 20},
      font: {family: 'Georgia, serif', size: 12},
      paper_bgcolor: 'white', plot_bgcolor: 'white',
      legend: {orientation: 'h', y: -0.18, font: {size: 11}},
    }, overrides || {});
    Plotly.newPlot(id, fig.data, layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
  });
}
loadPlotly('fig2a', 'assets/figures/fig2a_mia_loss_curves.json');
</script>
```

The result is close to chance. On SD 1.5, Rutkowski's mean loss is 0.277 and Bowater's is 0.246 — a gap of −0.031, meaning Rutkowski actually has *higher* loss than the control. The wrong direction, and a difference small enough to attribute to noise. This is not a failure of the experiment; it is the expected finding. [Dubinski et al. (WACV 2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12983) showed that at LAION scale, all standard membership inference attacks perform near chance. The model has seen five billion images; the effect of any single one on the weights is too small to detect. [Das et al. (ICML 2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.16201) showed that earlier apparent successes relied on comparing groups with different statistical properties, not genuine membership signal.

Open weights do not give the artist what they need. The trace is too faint to detect.

---

## Level 3: The model outputs

If the weights hide the trace, the outputs might reveal it. The question shifts: not whether the model *remembers* Rutkowski's images, but whether training on them created a learnable association between his name and his visual style. When you type "by Greg Rutkowski," does the model produce something that looks like his work?

We run two experiments that approach this from different angles.

### Name as content cue

The first uses BLIP, an image captioning model, to auto-generate a description for each of 30 Rutkowski portfolio images. The result is a content-focused prompt: something like "a painting of a knight in shining armor" or "a painting of a warrior on a dark battlefield." We then generate two images from SD 1.5 for each: one with the raw caption, one with ", by Greg Rutkowski" appended.

To measure similarity, we use CLIP ViT-L/14, which encodes both images and computes cosine similarity. CLIP is primarily content-sensitive — it picks up on subject matter more than visual style — so this test asks whether adding the name brings the generated image closer to the original in terms of what it depicts.

![Figure 3a](assets/figures/fig3a_caption_gallery.png){width=100% fig-alt="Figure 3a"}

::: {.fig-caption-block}
**Figure 3a.** Original paintings (left), SD 1.5 generation from the BLIP caption alone (center), and generation from the same caption with ", by Greg Rutkowski" appended (right). The five examples shown are the ones where adding the name produced the largest CLIP gain.
:::

Across 30 images, mean CLIP similarity increases from 0.704 (caption only) to 0.777 (caption + name), a gap of +0.073. The shift is consistent but modest: the name helps the model generate something more recognizable as the same subject, but content similarity was already fairly high without it.

### Name as style trigger

The second experiment is cleaner. We write 10 standardized prompts that all begin with "a painting of" — a knight, a dragon, a warrior, a sorceress, and so on — and generate 3 images per prompt from SD 1.5, once without any artist attribution and once with ", by Greg Rutkowski" appended. This gives 30 paired comparisons between the baseline and the named condition.

The measurement tool is **CSD (Content and Style Disentangled)**, introduced by [Somepalli et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.01292). Standard similarity metrics like CLIP conflate content and style. CSD is a ViT-L/14 model fine-tuned on LAION-Styles specifically to separate them: high CSD similarity between two images means they share the same visual language regardless of what they depict. We compute a prototype style vector from the 30 Rutkowski portfolio images and measure each generated image's cosine distance to that prototype — the **General Style Similarity (GSS)**.

```{=html}
<div class="plotly-wrap">
  <div id="fig3d"></div>
  <p class="fig-caption"><strong>Figure 3b.</strong> Mean CSD style similarity (GSS) to the Rutkowski prototype across 30 generated images (10 prompts × 3 seeds), with and without the artist name. Error bars show ±1 SD. Hover for exact values.</p>
</div>
<script>
loadPlotly('fig3d', 'assets/figures/fig3d_gss_barchart.json', {
  title: '',
  yaxis: {range: [0, 0.65], title: 'GSS (CSD cosine similarity)', tickfont: {size: 11}},
  showlegend: false,
  bargap: 0.5,
});
</script>
```

![Figure 3c](assets/figures/fig3c_style_gallery.png){width=100% fig-alt="Figure 3c"}

::: {.fig-caption-block}
**Figure 3c.** Caption only (top row) vs. caption + "by Greg Rutkowski" (bottom row), for five content prompts. Five prompts shown left to right: knight in armor, dragon, warrior, sorceress, castle at dusk.
:::

The effect is large. Without the name, GSS averages 0.249 — the model produces competent fantasy paintings with no particular stylistic signature. With the name, GSS rises to 0.530, more than doubling. Adding four words to the prompt nearly doubles the style fidelity of the output.

This is the clearest signal in the investigation. It means that when SD 1.5 trained on images captioned with Rutkowski's name, it did not just memorize those images — it learned a mapping from the name to a coherent visual style. The name now functions as a style key. You do not need to know what his paintings look like; you just need to know his name.

The Somepalli et al. paper reports GSS = 0.85 for Rutkowski on SD 1.4 using a direct "in the style of" prompt. Our 0.530 is lower, likely because our prompts begin with content descriptions rather than explicit style requests and our conditions are more controlled — but the scale of the effect is consistent. The name carries style information, and that information was encoded during training.

The legal catch is immediate. Style is not copyrightable. Section 102(b) of the US Copyright Act explicitly excludes "style or technique" from protection. A GSS of 0.530 does not establish infringement. So here we have the strongest signal in the investigation, and the one that matters least in court.

---

## But is reproduction even possible?

The three levels above ask a forensic question: given a model and an artist's portfolio, what evidence can we gather? But there is a separate question that matters more to the artist in practice: can someone sitting at a keyboard today, using commercially available models, actually replicate the artist's style on demand? And are those models trying to prevent it?

We test four models: **Gemini** (Google), **DALL-E 3** (OpenAI), **Midjourney**, and **Adobe Firefly**. Firefly is a meaningful addition: Adobe explicitly states it is trained only on licensed and public domain content, making it a potential negative control for style replication.

For each model, we run three kinds of experiments.

**Named style generation.** We ask directly for "a painting in the style of Greg Rutkowski." This is the most obvious test, and also the easiest for a provider to block. We document whether the model complies, refuses, adds a disclaimer, or silently modifies the output.

**Descriptive style generation.** We describe Rutkowski's visual characteristics without naming him: dramatic side-lighting, rich warm palette, painterly texture applied to fantastical subjects. This bypasses name-based filters. If the model still produces stylistically similar outputs, the style is encoded in the weights independent of name recognition.

**Zero-shot style transfer.** We upload one of Rutkowski's paintings and ask the model to generate a new image "in the style of this image," with no text attribution at all. The model receives the visual pattern as direct input and is asked to replicate it. This bypasses name-based filtering entirely: the model has no way of knowing whose work it is being shown.

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Awaiting manual experiments.** Run the three experiment types across all four models. See the notebook Section 5 for the full protocol. Save outputs to `assets/frontier/`.
:::

::: {.fig-placeholder}
**Figure 5a.** Named style generation across four models: what each produces (or refuses to produce) for "a painting in the style of Greg Rutkowski." Refusal text reproduced verbatim where applicable.
*Collect outputs manually and add to `assets/frontier/named/`.*
:::

::: {.fig-placeholder}
**Figure 5b.** Descriptive style generation across four models: outputs for the style description with no artist name. CSD similarity to Rutkowski's prototype annotated.
*Collect outputs manually and add to `assets/frontier/descriptive/`.*
:::

::: {.fig-placeholder}
**Figure 5c.** Zero-shot style transfer: outputs from each model given a Rutkowski painting as a visual reference. CSD similarity to his prototype annotated.
*Collect outputs manually and add to `assets/frontier/transfer/`.*
:::

::: {.fig-placeholder}
**Figure 5d.** CSD style similarity across all conditions (named, descriptive, zero-shot) and all models.
*Generate with notebook Section 5 after collecting all outputs.*
:::

**Refusal and behavior log:**

| Model | Experiment | Behavior |
|-------|------------|----------|
| Gemini | Named | *[TBD]* |
| Gemini | Descriptive | *[TBD]* |
| Gemini | Zero-shot transfer | *[TBD]* |
| DALL-E 3 | Named | *[TBD]* |
| DALL-E 3 | Descriptive | *[TBD]* |
| DALL-E 3 | Zero-shot transfer | *[TBD]* |
| Midjourney | Named | *[TBD]* |
| Midjourney | Descriptive | *[TBD]* |
| Adobe Firefly | Named | *[TBD]* |
| Adobe Firefly | Descriptive | *[TBD]* |

Two things are worth watching regardless of what the results show. First, whether blocking the artist's name actually prevents style replication. If a model refuses "in the style of Greg Rutkowski" but produces similar output from a description, the filter addresses a surface symptom, not the underlying capability. Second, whether the zero-shot transfer succeeds at all: if a model that refuses the named prompt will still faithfully replicate his style from a visual reference, then the refusal is purely about attribution, not about preventing the reproduction itself.

The refusals themselves carry a different kind of signal. Providers do not build name-based filters for artists they have never heard of. A filter implies awareness that this artist's name is associated with training data in a way that creates legal or reputational risk. That awareness is its own form of evidence.

---

## What can an artist actually know?

The answer is: more than nothing, but less than what the law requires.

At level 1, the finding is clear but the evidence is no longer independently verifiable. Rutkowski's work is in LAION-5B — this is established in the lawsuit record and confirmed by prior research. In copyright terms it establishes access, which is the beginning of an infringement claim, not the end. The LAION retrieval infrastructure that would let anyone verify this directly is no longer operational.

At level 2, the evidence fails. On SD 1.5, Rutkowski's mean denoising loss is 0.277 and Bowater's is 0.246 — a gap of −0.031 in the wrong direction. The model does not denoise his images more accurately than those of an artist not in its training data. Loss-based membership inference produces near-chance results at LAION scale, consistent with [Dubinski et al. (WACV 2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12983). Open weights give the artist very little more than a closed model would.

At level 3, the evidence is strongest and legally weakest. Adding "by Greg Rutkowski" to a prompt raises CLIP content similarity to his originals by 10% and CSD style similarity by 113% — from a GSS of 0.249 to 0.530. His name functions as a style key. The model has encoded a mapping from his name to his visual signature, and that mapping was built from training. But style is not copyrightable under Section 102(b) of the US Copyright Act. The strongest signal in the investigation is the one that matters least in court.

The inversion at the heart of this investigation is worth sitting with. The most accessible evidence — dataset presence — establishes the least legally. The most measurable evidence — style replication at the output level — is legally irrelevant. And the evidence that would actually matter in court — proof that a specific output copies a specific painting — is precisely what none of these methods can provide.

The UK High Court in *Getty v. Stability AI* (November 2025) rejected the argument that the trained model is itself an infringing copy. [Cooper and Grimmelmann (Chicago-Kent Law Review 2025)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.12590) argue that the real question is not whether a model remembers specific works but whether the process of learning from them creates liability. That question goes to trial in *Andersen v. Stability AI* in September 2026.

Greg Rutkowski's art is in the training data. The models trained on it can reproduce his style with high fidelity. He cannot, with currently available tools, prove that any specific output copies any specific painting. That is the gap, and it is where the lawsuits, the technology, and the law are still catching up to each other.
